skill.name: codebase-mapping
skill.description: Maps repository structure, import relationships, entry points, and key files so an assistant can understand an unfamiliar codebase before deeper analysis.
skill.summary: Generate a practical architecture map for a local repository.
skill.author: Dowwie

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported high-risk weak cryptography and environment-file patterns, but review found those were false positives from ordinary Markdown text and data keys. The confirmed risk is expected local filesystem access: the script recursively reads source files and can write a JSON report to a user-supplied path. No evidence found of network exfiltration, subprocess execution inside the script, malicious intent, or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Recursive Local Filesystem Analysis
medium_findings.description: The script walks a target repository and reads Python, JavaScript, and TypeScript files to extract imports. This is core functionality, but users should run it only on repositories they intend to disclose to the assistant.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly performs recursive local file traversal and parsing. This behavior is expected for the skill, so the risk is disclosure through normal use, not malicious activity.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Output File Write
medium_findings.description: The script writes the generated report to the path supplied with the output argument. This can overwrite an existing file if the user provides that path, although no hidden destination or exfiltration is present.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is direct and confirmed at the cited location. The destination is user controlled, which makes the issue operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Environment File Access
low_findings.description: The static finding points to logic that permits .env.example while skipping most hidden files. No evidence found that the script reads real .env files or environment secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding condition explicitly treats only .env.example as allowed. This supports a false-positive assessment for secret environment file access.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Patterns
low_findings.description: The reported lines contain data structure fields, descriptions, Markdown references, or argument parser text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic use at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of each cited line found no cryptographic API or hashing operation. The hits appear to be lexical matches in normal documentation and data fields.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backtick Command Execution
low_findings.description: Most external-command findings are Markdown code spans, file names, or fenced examples, not runtime execution by the skill. The only actual command is a documented user-invoked Python script example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited SKILL.md locations are documentation and examples rather than executable code. A user may run the documented Python command, but there is no shell injection pattern in the text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive File Reads
dangerous_patterns.description: The script traverses the selected repository and reads source files for import extraction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The traversal and file reads are explicit in the implementation. They are necessary for this analysis skill but should be disclosed.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The output path controls where the JSON report is written, which can overwrite a local file chosen by the user.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write operation and user-controlled path are directly visible. No network or hidden destination is present.

content.value_statement: Large repositories are hard to understand before the right files are known. This skill maps structure, dependencies, entry points, and key files for focused code review.
actual_capabilities: Generates a filtered repository tree that excludes common build and cache directories.
Extracts Python imports with the Python AST parser.
Extracts JavaScript and TypeScript import and require statements with pattern matching.
Identifies common Python and Node entry point files.
Groups likely key files for types, execution, tools, agents, and configuration.
Writes a structured JSON report or prints the report to standard output.
limitations: It analyzes only local files that the user makes available.
JavaScript and TypeScript dependency extraction uses regular expressions, not a full language parser.
It does not execute tests, build the project, or verify runtime behavior.
The output can include local file names and repository paths.

use_cases.title: Onboard to an unfamiliar repository
use_cases.description: Create a quick architecture map before reading implementation details.

use_cases.title: Prepare an architecture review
use_cases.description: Find entry points, package boundaries, and dependency clusters before deeper review.

use_cases.title: Scope AI-assisted code changes
use_cases.description: Identify relevant modules so Claude, Codex, or Claude Code can focus on the right files.

prompt_templates.title: Map this repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the codebase-mapping skill on this repository. Summarize the file tree, entry points, and most important modules.

prompt_templates.title: Find dependency boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Map this codebase and identify internal dependency groups, external packages, and likely module boundaries. Highlight areas that need closer review.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a focused refactor plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use codebase mapping to locate entry points, execution files, configuration files, and shared types. Then propose a focused refactor investigation path.

prompt_templates.title: Compare architecture signals
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a codebase map, classify dependencies by area, and explain how the structure supports or blocks maintainability, testing, and future changes.

output_examples.input: Map a Python service before adding a new API endpoint.
output: A concise repository tree with cache and build folders excluded.
Likely entry points such as application modules, CLI modules, and project configuration.
A short list of key files to inspect before implementing the endpoint.

output_examples.input: Review a mixed TypeScript and Python repository for architecture boundaries.
output: A dependency overview separating internal modules from third-party packages.
Detected frontend, backend, configuration, and shared type areas.
Questions for follow-up review where boundaries are unclear.

output_examples.input: Prepare context for Claude Code before a refactor.
output: The files most likely to control execution, configuration, and shared interfaces.
A suggested reading order for understanding the affected modules.
Known limits of the static map that need manual confirmation.

best_practices: Run the skill from the repository root for the clearest relative paths.
Review generated paths before sharing output outside your organization.
Use the map as a starting point, then inspect the important files directly.
anti_patterns: Do not run it on repositories that contain secrets you cannot share with the assistant.
Do not treat the dependency graph as complete for dynamic imports or generated code.
Do not overwrite an important file with the output path.

faq.question: Does this skill modify source files?
faq.answer: No. It reads source files and writes an optional separate analysis report.

faq.question: Does it send repository data to a network service?
faq.answer: No evidence found of network requests in the reviewed files.

faq.question: Which languages does it analyze?
faq.answer: It focuses on Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, JSX, and TSX files.

faq.question: Can it find every dependency?
faq.answer: No. Static analysis can miss dynamic imports, generated files, and framework conventions.

faq.question: Why does it have a medium risk rating?
faq.answer: It recursively reads local repository files and can write a report to a user-selected path.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this output?
faq.answer: Yes. The output is designed to help AI coding assistants focus on relevant files and boundaries.
